subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8! In this unit you will explore angles – one of the most useful ideas in all of geometry. An angle is simply a measure of turn, and once you start looking for angles you will find them absolutely everywhere. There is an angle in the corner of your exercise book, an angle between the hands of a clock, an angle where the wall meets the floor, and even an angle in your arm when you bend your elbow. When Amina opens a door, when Yusuf kicks a football towards the goal, and when Halima folds a sheet of anjero in half, they are all making and using angles.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning, which means you move forward at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when the idea feels clear. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky. There is no rush – understanding matters far more than speed.

Why do angles matter so much? Angles are the language of shape and direction. A builder in Hargeisa uses right angles to make sure the walls of a house stand up straight and true. A carpenter uses angles to cut the sloping timbers of a roof so the rain runs off. When you give someone directions – “turn right at the masjid, then a small turn towards the market” – you are describing turns, and every turn is an angle. Footballers judge the angle of a pass, tailors judge the angle of a fold, and navigators at sea judge the angle of their course. Learning about angles now gives you a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and it prepares you for the more advanced geometry you will meet in later years.

As you work, keep a pencil, a ruler, and a small square of paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. The corner of that square of paper is a perfect right angle, and you will use it again and again as your own personal angle checker. Draw the angles, turn the page around, and say the names out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next.
Understand an angle as a measure of turn and name its vertex and arms.
Recognise and identify right angles in the environment around you.
Compare angles by size using the words greater than, less than and equal to.
Identify and name the types of angle: acute, right, obtuse and straight.
Recognise that an angle keeps its type in any orientation it is turned to.
Understand that angles are measured in degrees, and know a full turn is 360°.
Identify the angles in 2D shapes and count the right angles a shape contains.

concepts.title: What Is an Angle?
concepts.explanation: An angle is a measure of turn. Imagine you are standing in one spot in the school yard at Nuur Academy, facing the gate. Now turn your body to face the masjid. The amount you turned – how far your body swung around – is an angle. A small turn makes a small angle. A large turn makes a large angle. The angle is not about how far you walked; it is only about how much you turned. We can also make an angle without moving at all. An angle is formed whenever two straight lines meet at a point. That meeting point is…
concepts.example: Here is a simple picture of an angle. The letter O marks the vertex, and the two lines stretching away from it are the arms:

concepts.title: The Right Angle – Your Special Reference
concepts.explanation: Of all the angles, the right angle is the most important to know by heart, because you will use it as your measuring stick for every other angle. A right angle is a square corner. It measures exactly 90 degrees, and it looks like the corner of your exercise book, the corner of a window, or the corner of a prayer mat. Right angles are all around you. Here are just a few places to find them:
concepts.example: The four corners of your maths book, a sheet of paper, or a door.

concepts.title: Comparing Angles by Size
concepts.explanation: To compare two angles means to decide which one is bigger, which is smaller, or whether they are the same. Because an angle is the amount of opening between its arms, the angle that is more open is the greater one, and the angle that is more closed is the smaller one. We use three familiar words and symbols to describe how angles compare: Greater than (>): the angle is wider or more open.
concepts.example: Less than (<): the angle is narrower or more closed.

concepts.title: The Types of Angle
concepts.explanation: Now that you can compare any angle to a right angle, you are ready to learn the proper names mathematicians use. Angles are sorted into types based on their size. There are four types you need to know well in Year 4. A right angle measures exactly 90 degrees. It is the square corner you already know so well, and it is the boundary line between the two most common types below.
concepts.example: An obtuse angle is bigger than a right angle but smaller than a straight line – that is, more than 90 degrees but less than 180 degrees. Obtuse angles look wide open and blunt. A partly reclined chair, an open laptop,…

concepts.title: Orientation and Degrees
concepts.explanation: Here is an idea that trips up many learners, so read it carefully. Turning an angle around does not change its type. An angle is decided by how much its arms are opened, not by which way it is pointing. A right angle is still a right angle whether its corner points up, down, left, right, or lies on its side. The same is true for acute and obtuse angles. Think about the corner of a set square or a piece of card. You can rotate that card any way you like – lay it flat, stand it on a point, tilt it sideways – and the…
concepts.example: It is also good to know how angles are measured. Angles are measured in units called degrees, written with a small circle like this: 90°. A complete full turn, all the way around back to the start, is 360 degrees. From…

concepts.title: Angles in 2D Shapes
concepts.explanation: Every flat, two-dimensional shape has angles, and they appear at the corners where the sides meet. Each corner is called a vertex, and studying the angles at these corners tells us a great deal about the shape. Let us look at some common shapes. A square has four sides and four corners, and every single corner is a right angle. So a square has 4 right angles. A rectangle also has four sides and four corners, and just like the square, every corner is a right angle, so a rectangle has 4 right angles too. This is one…
concepts.example: A triangle has three sides and three corners, and its angles can be different types depending on the triangle. An equilateral triangle, where all three sides are equal, has three equal acute angles of 60° each, so it…

methods.title: How to name the type of an angle
methods.example: In an angle, what do we call the point where the two arms meet, and what do we call the two lines themselves?
steps: Compare the angle to a right angle using a paper corner.
If it is smaller than the square corner, it is acute.
If it matches the square corner exactly, it is a right angle.
If it is wider than the square corner but not a flat line, it is obtuse.
If it is a flat straight line, it is a straight angle.

methods.title: How to count right angles in a shape
methods.example: The hands of a clock at 5:00 make an angle. Is it less than, equal to, or greater than a right angle?
steps: Look at each corner (vertex) of the shape one at a time.
Hold a paper corner into each angle to test it.
Mark every corner that matches the square corner exactly.
Count your marks – that is the number of right angles.

methods.title: How to work out a turn direction
methods.example: The angle in the letter V – is it acute, right, obtuse or straight?
steps: Start by facing the given direction (for example North).
A quarter turn moves you to the next compass point (North to East, clockwise).
A half turn moves you to the opposite direction (North to South).
Name the angle: quarter turn = right angle, half turn = straight angle.

methods.title: Orientation does not change the type
methods.example: Zahra draws a right angle, then turns her paper so the corner points downwards. Is it still a right angle?
steps: Solution: Yes.
Turning the paper changes only the orientation, not the opening between the arms.
The angle is still exactly 90°, so it is still a right angle.

methods.title: Count the right angles in a shape
methods.example: How many right angles does a rectangle have?
steps: Solution: A rectangle has four corners, and every corner is a square corner.
So a rectangle has 4 right angles.
Check the count the right angles in a shape result carefully.

methods.title: Turning and direction
methods.example: Idris is facing North. He makes a quarter turn clockwise. Which direction is he now facing, and what type of angle did he turn through?
steps: Solution: A quarter turn clockwise from North brings him to face East.
A quarter turn is 90°, so he turned through a right angle.
Check the turning and direction result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Name the parts of an angle
workedExamples.prompt: In an angle, what do we call the point where the two arms meet, and what do we call the two lines themselves?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The point where the two arms meet is the vertex. The two straight lines are the arms of the angle. Remember, the angle is the amount of opening at the vertex, not the length of the arms.

workedExamples.title: Compare an angle to a right angle
workedExamples.prompt: The hands of a clock at 5:00 make an angle. Is it less than, equal to, or greater than a right angle?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: At 5:00 the hands are opened wider than a square corner, so the angle is greater than a right angle. That makes it an obtuse angle.

workedExamples.title: Identify the type of angle
workedExamples.prompt: The angle in the letter V – is it acute, right, obtuse or straight?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The letter V is sharp and narrow, much smaller than a square corner. It is less than 90°, so it is an acute angle.

workedExamples.title: Orientation does not change the type
workedExamples.prompt: Zahra draws a right angle, then turns her paper so the corner points downwards. Is it still a right angle?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Yes. Turning the paper changes only the orientation, not the opening between the arms. The angle is still exactly 90°, so it is still a right angle.

workedExamples.title: Count the right angles in a shape
workedExamples.prompt: How many right angles does a rectangle have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A rectangle has four corners, and every corner is a square corner. So a rectangle has 4 right angles.

workedExamples.title: Turning and direction
workedExamples.prompt: Idris is facing North. He makes a quarter turn clockwise. Which direction is he now facing, and what type of angle did he turn through?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A quarter turn clockwise from North brings him to face East. A quarter turn is 90°, so he turned through a right angle.

workedExamples.title: A straight angle
workedExamples.prompt: Halima turns all the way from facing forward to facing directly backward, in one smooth movement. What type of angle has she turned through?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Facing directly backward is a half turn, which is 180°. A half turn is a straight angle.

workedExamples.title: Angles in a triangle
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has one right angle. What can you say about its other two angles?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: In this right-angled triangle, the other two angles must both be acute (each less than 90°). They cannot be right angles or obtuse, because a triangle cannot hold two angles that big together with the right angle.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Compare each angle to a right angle. Write greater than, less than, or equal to: a) the letter V b) the corner of a ruler c) an open laptop.
workedExamples.solution: a) less than, b) equal to, c) greater than.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: You are facing North. Name the direction you face after: a) a quarter turn clockwise b) a half turn c) three quarter turns clockwise.
workedExamples.solution: a) East, b) South, c) West.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Draw and label one acute angle, one right angle and one obtuse angle.
workedExamples.solution: Check that the acute angle is smaller than a square corner, the right angle matches it, and the obtuse angle is wider.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A regular hexagon has six equal angles of 120° each. What type are they, and how many right angles does the hexagon have?
workedExamples.solution: 1) Obtuse (120° is more than 90°); 0 right angles.

activities.title: Make a Paper-Corner Angle Checker
activities.materials: any sheet of paper and your two hands.
steps: Take a sheet of paper – each corner is a perfect right angle.
Walk around your home and hold the corner into ten different angles: door frames, window corners, table corners, the point of a knife.
For each one, decide: is it less than, equal to, or greater than a right angle?
Write a list of the acute, right and obtuse angles you found.
Keep your paper-corner checker – you can use it all through this unit as your personal angle tool.

activities.title: Angle Hunt Around the Home
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and sharp eyes.
steps: Make three columns headed Acute, Right and Obtuse.
Search your home and yard for at least two examples of each type of angle.
Write where you found each one – for example, ‘acute: the point of a slice of mango’.
Explain to a family member why each angle belongs in its column.

activities.title: Body Angles Game
activities.materials: just yourself and some space to move.
steps: Stand up straight and face a wall. This is your starting direction.
Make a quarter turn and name the angle you turned through (a right angle).
Make a half turn and name it (a straight angle); make a full turn and name it (a full turn).
Now use your arms: make an acute angle, a right angle, and an obtuse angle with your two arms, like a human protractor.

activities.title: The Clock Angle Investigation
activities.materials: a clock with hands, or a paper clock you draw yourself.
steps: Set the clock to 3:00 and look at the angle between the hands. What type is it?
Move the hands to 1:00, then 5:00, then 6:00, naming the angle each time.
Find two times when the hands make an acute angle, and two times when they make an obtuse angle.
Investigate: at how many o’clock times do the hands make exactly a right angle?

activities.title: Shape Angle Detective
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, and a pencil.
steps: Draw a square, a rectangle, an equilateral triangle, a right-angled triangle and a pentagon.
Use your paper-corner checker on every corner of each shape.
Mark each right angle with a small square, and label the acute and obtuse angles.
Count and record how many right angles each shape has.

activities.title: Design Challenge (Family or Group Task)
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, and coloured pencils.
steps: Design a robot or a house using only straight lines.
Include at least 3 right angles, at least 2 acute angles, and at least 2 obtuse angles.
Label every angle in your design with its type.
Show your design to your family and explain why each angle is acute, right or obtuse.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand the types of angle best? What was the trickiest part? Which angles were hardest to tell apart, and how did you decide? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: The Four Types of Angle Acute: less than 90 degrees (small and sharp). Right: exactly 90 degrees (a square corner). Obtuse: more than 90 but less than 180 degrees (wide and blunt). Straight: exactly 180 degrees (a flat straight line, a half turn).

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Turns and Degrees A quarter turn = 90 degrees = a right angle. A half turn = 180 degrees = a straight angle. A full turn = 360 degrees, all the way around.

rules.title: The Golden Rule
rules.text: of Angles The length of the arms never changes the size of an angle. Only the amount of opening between the arms matters. Turning an angle around does not change its type.

terms: The amount of turn between two arms
The point where the two arms meet
The two straight lines forming the angle
commonMistakes: Longer arms mean a bigger angle
Only the opening between the arms counts
A turned right angle becomes acute
Rotating an angle never changes its type
A rhombus has no right angles; always test
The vertex is only the meeting point

realProblems.prompt: At the Hargeisa market, Omar builds a square stall. He checks all four corners with a set square. How many right angles should he find if the stall is truly square?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Ibrahim faces East. He makes a half turn to walk back home. Which direction is home, and what type of angle did he turn through?
realProblems.answer: West; a half turn is a straight angle.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: West; a half turn is a straight angle.

realProblems.prompt: A clock shows 3:00, then later 5:00. Describe how the angle between the hands changes from one time to the other.
realProblems.answer: At 3:00 the hands make a right angle; by 5:00 they have opened wider into an obtuse angle.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: At 3:00 the hands make a right angle; by 5:00 they have opened wider into an obtuse angle.

realProblems.prompt: Aisha designs a pattern using shapes. She wants a shape with no right angles at all. Name two shapes she could use.
realProblems.answer: Any two of: circle (no angles), equilateral triangle, regular pentagon, regular hexagon, rhombus.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Any two of: circle (no angles), equilateral triangle, regular pentagon, regular hexagon, rhombus.

realProblems.prompt: Liban walks North, then makes a quarter turn clockwise, walks on, then makes another quarter turn clockwise. Which direction is he facing at the end, and what is the total angle he has turned through?
realProblems.answer: He faces East; two quarter turns make a half turn, which is 180° (a straight angle).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He faces East; two quarter turns make a half turn, which is 180° (a straight angle).

realProblems.prompt: A regular hexagon has six equal angles of 120° each. What type are they, and how many right angles does the hexagon have?
realProblems.answer: 1) Obtuse (120° is more than 90°); 0 right angles.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Obtuse (120° is more than 90°); 0 right angles.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A regular hexagon has six equal angles of 120° each. What type are they, and how many right angles does the hexagon have?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) Obtuse (120° is more than 90°); 0 right angles.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A triangle has one right angle. Explain what must be true about the other two angles.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Both other angles must be acute (each less than 90°), because the shape already has one right angle.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Zahra says, ‘If I turn a right angle upside down it becomes an acute angle.’ Is she right? Explain your answer.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: No – turning it upside down changes only the orientation, not the opening, so it stays a right angle.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: The letter E is made of straight lines. How many right angles can you find in a capital letter E?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A capital letter E has 8 right angles (where the horizontal bars meet the vertical stroke and at the ends).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Orientation and Degrees.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Here is an idea that trips up many learners, so read it carefully. Turning an angle around does not change its type. An angle is decided by how much its arms are opened, not by which way it is pointing. A right angle is still a right angle whether its corner points up, down, left, right, or lies on its side. The same is true for acute and obtuse angles. Think about the corner of a set square or a piece of card. You can rotate that card any way you like – lay it flat, stand it on a point, tilt it sideways – and the…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Angles in 2D Shapes.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Every flat, two-dimensional shape has angles, and they appear at the corners where the sides meet. Each corner is called a vertex, and studying the angles at these corners tells us a great deal about the shape. Let us look at some common shapes. A square has four sides and four corners, and every single corner is a right angle. So a square has 4 right angles. A rectangle also has four sides and four corners, and just like the square, every corner is a right angle, so a rectangle has 4 right angles too. This is one…

The point where the two arms meet
The two straight lines forming the angle

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning, which means you move forward at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when the idea feels clear. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky. There is no rush – understanding matters far more than speed.

I can why do angles matter so much? Angles are the language of shape and direction. A builder in Hargeisa uses right angles to make sure the walls of a house stand up straight and true. A carpenter uses angles to cut the sloping timbers of a roof so the rain runs off. When you give someone directions – “turn right at the masjid, then a small turn towards the market” – you are describing turns, and every turn is an angle. Footballers judge the angle of a pass, tailors judge the angle of a fold, and navigators at sea judge the angle of their course. Learning about angles now gives you a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and it prepares you for the more advanced geometry you will meet in later years.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, a ruler, and a small square of paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. The corner of that square of paper is a perfect right angle, and you will use it again and again as your own personal angle checker. Draw the angles, turn the page around, and say the names out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next.
I can understand an angle as a measure of turn and name its vertex and arms.
I can recognise and identify right angles in the environment around you.
I can compare angles by size using the words greater than, less than and equal to.
I can identify and name the types of angle: acute, right, obtuse and straight.
I can recognise that an angle keeps its type in any orientation it is turned to.
